item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image is from here, which is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff. Although him leaving his wife was a dick move.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Charybdis? is that you? This is really cool. Upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for necroposting, but wrong Old Gods.. Although, now that I get to thinking about it, Greek mythology is pretty Lovecraftian. Also, the link is broken. Here's a not-broken link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contrary to most, I found it boring. Downvote.

I liked the canyon description, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this in many different ways. The Greek mythology reference is well done, as is the writing. The "this thing is bad, but what it is holding in check is worse" is right up Foundation Alley. And the implied remorse on the part of Charybdis pushed it beyond the plus (which I don't hand out willy-nilly).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the gods fight, what power do mere mortals have?

Definitely like it, some craziness going on, with people being pretty powerless to do much of anything about it all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not necessarily. When something horrible is going on but nobody knows what it is or where it's coming from, our curiosity tends to get us killed. Also people panic and do dumb shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not too shabby! I dig anything based off Greek mythology, especially something like Charybdis which I always thought was kind of neat. There was one part during the interview that left me scratching my head, though. The boat starts shaking, he thinks it's a hurricane, and everyone…runs outside? What? And his wife gets hit by a food cart…on a cruise ship? I mean I guess they have carts, but it's not like an airplane. And how can he "not reach her" exactly? She's on the ground bleeding after getting hit by a food cart; the least he could do is bend down and pick her up. And if the boat is rocking so hard as to send murderous carts everywhere, how is anyone running and not thrown to the ground?

Anyway, that part just bugged me because I had no idea what was going on or why. Maybe if it was just a loud roaring sound of the churning water and people ran outside to see, trampling his wife in the process? It just seems odd because most people's reactions to something horrible going on outside would be to freeze and cower. But outside of that one gripe, the rest is good enough to earn an upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object classification is based on difficulty of containment, not necessarily capability of destruction. I mean, a nuclear bomb has a huge potential for destruction, but under the Foundation class system it would be considered Safe because you could lock in it in a room and nothing would happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't actually get this was Charypdis and Schylla, but it works great either way. The description of the canyon is surreal and creepy, although I agree the father's dialogue sounds a bit unconvincing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well done. My reactions in order:

So what, it's just a moving whirlpool
That's stupid, how would he still be alive
That's kind of sad, but it's still just a whirlpool
COOL

In other words, that canyon thing really made it for me. Really got me thinking. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a fan of greek mythology, i love this. And i feel a little pride at calling it. This is fantastically done. I loved the addendum.

Was the son's name a reference to the Argonauts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just enough here for an upvote. The final addendum sparked some minor thoughts that were confirmed by the spoilers, so I'm glad to see I was steered in the right direction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough; those are all reasons to support Keter classification.

Still, the thing has been around a while and doesn't seem to be enough of a threat to bring down humanity as a whole, nor does destroying civilization seem to be its goal. When I saw the classification, I assumed it'd have some effect on weather patterns that might be enough to disrupt climate on wide scales, something like that.

Of course, there are hints that maybe this thing took down Atlantis…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that. The dialog is even a bit better now! Plus'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The use of "incurred" bugs the shit out of me, but I have trouble coming up with a suitable replacement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rather that it's uncontainable, indestructible, and kills people despite the Foundation's best efforts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a wormhole, the opposite side of the Bermuda Triangle, and Charybdis all in one? I like it.

But I'm not certain it's a Keter. Was that decision based on the living thing that may come out of it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is the most well composed of your SCPs yet. Glad you decided to take my advice regarding the redaction, I like this version much better.

+1. "Your skills have improved, Fox!" ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it! One problem though…

Dr. Adler: What year do believe it is?

Just noticed that. Still, love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I stand corrected. Sorry. Skimmed that part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I patched up the dialogue a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It sank the titanic

No it didn't. The Titanic sank off the coast of Newfoundland, not Norway, and this is a fishing boat.

EDIT: Sorry if my response was irate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It sank the Titanic and the dialogue doesn't feel convincing at all.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like this, but the dialog in the interview seems off. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Messenger of Fear in sight, DARK DECEPTION kills the liiight...

Hybrid-children watch-the-sea, pray for Father roaming free!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great, classic (lol) stuff.

One thing though. At the end of the interview, he says "…It greeted me once more…" Tonally, this is very jarring, as his speech patterns to that point have been contemporary. I think I understand why it's there, but I'd still strongly recommend changing the phrasing to something less melodramatic.

Regardless, an enthusiastic +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I thought that was the inspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always been terrified by whirlpools, and this one does a very good job at exploiting that.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's standing on an edge inside the canyon, like it juts out from the canyon wall.

+ Spoilers
-And So Forth

Charybdis was commanded by Poseidon to stop Scylla from doing something, and Charybdis is unable to fully contain Scylla.

I'll see if I can add to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You always seem to give me the chills with your Keter SCPs. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I'm agreeing with Scantron about the final addendum. It's almost content-less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it, except for two parts:

  • How was he standing in the canyon if he couldn't see the bottom? Maybe I'm just not imagining it right.
  • I don't see what the final addendum has to do with anything; the article could either do to have it expanded or removed.
